prompt: Which blood vessel carries oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium?

options.text: Pulmonary veins
Pulmonary arteries
Superior vena cava
Coronary sinus only

explanation: Pulmonary veins return oxygen-rich blood to the left atrium; pulmonary arteries carry deoxygenated blood to the lungs.

distractorExplanations.b: Pulmonary arteries go to the lungs with deoxygenated blood.
distractorExplanations.d: Coronary sinus drains heart venous blood.

prompt: The outermost layer of a vein/artery wall is the:

options.text: Tunica intima only
Tunica externa (adventitia)
Myocardium
Endocardium

explanation: Vessels have intima, media, and externa; myocardium/endocardium are heart layers.

prompt: Which formed element is primarily responsible for clotting?

options.text: Erythrocytes only
Plasma albumin only
Platelets (thrombocytes)
Lymphocytes only

explanation: Platelets form the primary hemostatic plug; coagulation factors in plasma complete clotting.

prompt: Serum differs from plasma in that serum:

options.text: Always contains fibrinogen like plasma
Is only obtained from arterial blood
Cannot be used for any chemistry tests
Lacks clotting factors consumed when blood clots (no anticoagulant used)

explanation: Plasma is from anticoagulated blood; serum is from clotted blood after factors are consumed.

distractorExplanations.a: Fibrinogen is in plasma, not serum.

prompt: The antecubital fossa veins commonly used for venipuncture include:

options.text: Median cubital, cephalic, and basilic veins
Aorta, carotid, and femoral arteries
Portal vein only
Pulmonary trunk only

explanation: Standard AC veins are median cubital, cephalic, and basilic; arteries are avoided for routine venipuncture.

distractorExplanations.b: Those are arteries/major vessels not for routine draws.

prompt: Hemostasis refers to:

options.text: Destruction of all leukocytes
The process that stops bleeding
Measurement of hemoglobin only
Forced hemolysis in the tube

explanation: Hemostasis includes vascular spasm, platelet plug, and coagulation.

prompt: Which chamber pumps blood into the systemic circulation?

options.text: Right atrium
Right ventricle
Left ventricle
Left atrium

explanation: Left ventricle ejects into the aorta; right ventricle pumps to pulmonary arteries.

prompt: Capillaries are specialized for:

options.text: High-pressure elastic storage only like the aorta
Preventing all fluid exchange
Generating electrical impulses like the SA node
Exchange of gases, nutrients, and wastes with tissues

explanation: Thin capillary walls enable exchange; arteries are conduit/pressure vessels.

distractorExplanations.a: Aorta is a large elastic artery.

prompt: Leukocytes function mainly in:

options.text: Immune defense against infection
Oxygen transport exclusively
Clot retraction exclusively
Producing bile

explanation: WBCs defend against pathogens; oxygen is RBC/hemoglobin role.

prompt: Why are veins preferred over arteries for routine blood collection?

options.text: Veins always carry oxygenated blood
Thinner walls, lower pressure, and more accessible superficial locations
Arteries have no pulse risk if punctured
Veins never bruise

explanation: Venipuncture targets veins for safety and accessibility; arterial puncture is specialized.

prompt: Which vessel returns deoxygenated blood from the head/arms region toward the heart?

options.text: Pulmonary vein from the lungs to the left atrium as a systemic vein from the arm
Aorta as a vein
Superior vena cava (via venous pathways from upper body)
Coronary sinus as the antecubital draw site

explanation: Upper-body systemic venous return reaches the right atrium via the SVC; pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood.

distractorExplanations.a: Pulmonary veins are not arm venipuncture anatomy.
distractorExplanations.d: Coronary sinus is cardiac venous drainage, not AC draw.

prompt: The antecubital fossa commonly used for venipuncture contains:

options.text: Only arteries and no veins
Only lymphatic ducts suitable for vacuum tubes
The spinal cord
Median cubital, cephalic, and basilic veins as common options

explanation: Phlebotomists select among AC veins based on accessibility and safety (basilic near nerves/brachial artery).

distractorExplanations.b: Lymphatics are not routine blood draw sites.
distractorExplanations.c: Spinal cord is not in the AC fossa.

prompt: Which statement about capillaries is correct?

options.text: They are microscopic vessels where exchange of gases/nutrients occurs and are the source of dermal puncture blood mixtures
They are the largest arteries in the body
They contain only valves like large veins always
They are never involved in heel-stick specimens

explanation: Capillary beds enable exchange; skin punctures collect capillary blood (with interstitial/arterial mix).

distractorExplanations.c: Capillaries lack the valve anatomy of veins.

prompt: Blood pressure is highest in which vessel type under normal physiology?

options.text: Veins of the foot
Arteries (especially aorta/large arteries)
Capillaries of the skin only
Lymphatic capillaries

explanation: Arterial pressure is highest; veins are low-pressure capacitance vessels.

distractorExplanations.c: Capillary pressure is lower than arterial.
distractorExplanations.d: Lymphatics are not systemic arterial pressure.

prompt: The basilic vein is often considered higher risk for venipuncture because it lies near:

options.text: Only the Achilles tendon
The trachea
The brachial artery and median nerve
The femoral head exclusively

explanation: Medial AC anatomy places basilic near artery/nerve—use caution or choose safer veins first.

prompt: Which tube additive is in a lavender-top tube used for CBC?

options.text: EDTA
Sodium citrate
Sodium fluoride
No additive (serum tube)

explanation: EDTA anticoagulates by chelating calcium and preserves cell morphology for hematology.

prompt: The correct CLSI order of draw begins with:

options.text: Lavender first always
Blood culture bottles/SPS when cultures are ordered, then other tubes per sequence
Gray first always
Any random order

explanation: Cultures first reduce contamination; then follow citrate, serum, heparin, EDTA, glycolytic inhibitor sequences.

distractorExplanations.a: EDTA first can contaminate other tests.
distractorExplanations.c: Gray is typically last among common tubes.

prompt: A butterfly (winged infusion) set is most useful when:

options.text: Collecting only arterial blood gases always
Replacing the need for patient ID
Drawing from small/fragile veins or hand veins
Drawing 20 large tubes faster than a straight needle in easy veins

explanation: Butterflies help difficult/small veins but increase cost and hemolysis risk if misused.

distractorExplanations.d: Easy AC veins often use straight needles efficiently.

prompt: Gauge number relationship to needle diameter is:

options.text: Higher gauge = larger diameter always
Gauge unrelated to diameter
25G is larger than 18G
Higher gauge = smaller diameter lumen

explanation: 21–23G are common for venipuncture; higher gauge means thinner needle.

prompt: Why must light-blue citrate tubes be filled completely?

options.text: To maintain the required 9:1 blood-to-anticoagulant ratio
To dilute coagulation factors intentionally
Because underfilling speeds clotting usefully
Citrate volume is decorative

explanation: Underfilled citrate tubes falsely prolong PT/aPTT.

distractorExplanations.b: Dilution from excess citrate harms accuracy.

prompt: A gray-top tube with sodium fluoride is commonly used for:

options.text: STAT electrolytes only as first choice
Glucose testing when glycolysis inhibition is needed
Blood bank crossmatch exclusively
ESR testing exclusively

explanation: Fluoride inhibits glycolysis preserving glucose; oxalate/EDTA variants exist by manufacturer.

prompt: Tourniquet application should generally not exceed:

options.text: Ten minutes routinely
Thirty minutes
About one minute before the draw
No time limit

explanation: Prolonged tourniquet time causes hemoconcentration and analyte changes.

prompt: Which action reduces needle needlestick risk after a draw?

options.text: Recapping with two hands
Leaving the needle on the bed for later
Bending the needle before trash disposal
Activating the engineered safety feature immediately into a sharps container

explanation: OSHA-aligned practice forbids two-handed recapping and improper disposal.

prompt: Serum separator tubes (SST) contain:

options.text: Clot activator and gel separator
EDTA only
Sodium citrate only
Heparin and citrate together always

explanation: SST allows clotting then gel barrier separation of serum after centrifugation.

distractorExplanations.d: Mixed anticoagulants are not SST design.

prompt: When transferring blood from a syringe into vacuum tubes, the phlebotomist should:

options.text: Force blood through a needle into tubes under high pressure
Use a transfer device and allow vacuum to fill tubes without forcing
Remove stoppers and pour quickly while shaking
Stick the needle through the finger to vent air

explanation: Forcing causes hemolysis; transfer devices improve safety.

prompt: A butterfly (winged infusion) set is most helpful when:

options.text: Replacing the need for patient identification
Drawing arterial blood gases without training
Drawing from small/fragile veins or hand veins with better control
Avoiding order of draw rules

explanation: Butterflies aid difficult veins; ID and order of draw still apply; ABGs need specialized training.

prompt: Which needle gauge has a larger lumen?

options.text: 25-gauge compared with 18-gauge
Gauge number equal to lumen size directly
30-gauge is always preferred for blood bank units
18-gauge (smaller gauge number = larger diameter)

explanation: Lower gauge numbers are larger bores; very small gauges increase hemolysis risk for some tests.

distractorExplanations.c: Blood bank often uses larger-bore needles.

prompt: Serum separator tubes (SST, often gold/tiger) typically:

options.text: Contain clot activator and gel for serum chemistry after clotting/centrifugation
Contain EDTA for CBC
Contain sodium citrate for PT/INR
Must never be inverted after draw

explanation: SST yields serum for many chemistry tests; invert gently to mix clot activator.

prompt: A gray-top tube with sodium fluoride/potassium oxalate is commonly used for:

options.text: CBC only
Glucose testing when glycolysis inhibition is needed
Coagulation PT/INR only
Blood cultures exclusively

explanation: Fluoride inhibits glycolysis; gray tops are classic glucose/alcohol (policy) tubes—not CBC/coag/cultures.

prompt: When using a syringe draw transferred to evacuated tubes, the phlebotomist should:

options.text: Squirt blood forcefully into tubes with the needle uncapped toward coworkers
Recap the needle with two hands then puncture stoppers blindly
Transfer using a transfer device—never force blood into tubes by removing stoppers with the needle tip in an unsafe way
Discard the order of draw because syringe draws are exempt

explanation: Safety transfer devices prevent needlesticks; order of draw still applies when filling tubes.

prompt: Specimen tubes should be labeled:

options.text: At the bedside immediately after collection with two identifiers per policy
In the lab break room hours later from memory
Before calling the patient into the room using charts only
With room number as the only identifier

explanation: Bedside labeling prevents wrong-patient errors.

prompt: A specimen for cold agglutinin testing may require:

options.text: Freezing immediately in a household freezer
Warming/transport at body temperature per lab instructions
Leaving at outdoor winter temperatures
No special handling ever

explanation: Some analytes require temperature control (warm, chilled, protected from light).

distractorExplanations.c: Extreme cold may be wrong for that test.

prompt: Photosensitive analytes (e.g., bilirubin) should be:

options.text: Left under bright windows intentionally
Heated on a radiator
Protected from light during transport
Shaken continuously for 30 minutes

explanation: Light degrades certain analytes; amber tubes or wrapping are used.

prompt: Hemoconcentration from prolonged tourniquet can falsely elevate:

options.text: Nothing; values never change
Only urine specific gravity
Oxygen saturation on pulse ox only
Proteins and protein-bound substances (and contribute to potassium issues)

explanation: Plasma water leaves the vein, concentrating cells/proteins.

distractorExplanations.c: Pulse ox is not a tube analyte.

prompt: If a specimen is lipemic, it means:

options.text: Cloudy/milky serum/plasma often from recent fat intake or metabolic causes
The sample is sterile surgical fluid
It contains only water
It was drawn from an artery

explanation: Lipemia can interfere with optical assays; fasting may be required.

distractorExplanations.d: Route is not defined by lipemia.

prompt: Chain of custody is most critical for:

options.text: Routine cholesterol screens only
Forensic/toxicology specimens where legal integrity must be documented
Discarded empty tubes
Patient magazines in waiting rooms

explanation: COC documents handling for legal acceptability of results.

distractorExplanations.a: Routine chemistry rarely needs full COC.

prompt: Centrifugation of a serum tube should occur:

options.text: Immediately while still liquid without clotting time
After freezing solid
After the specimen has fully clotted per tube instructions
Only if the patient requests it

explanation: Premature spin of serum tubes can trap fibrin and harm quality.

distractorExplanations.a: Spinning too early causes fibrin issues.
distractorExplanations.b: Freezing before processing is usually wrong.
distractorExplanations.d: Lab protocol decides, not patient request.

prompt: A rejected specimen due to hemolysis should lead the phlebotomist to:

options.text: Report hemolyzed potassium as accurate without comment
Pour the hemolyzed blood into a new tube to 'fix' it
Shake harder next time
Recollect properly after reviewing technique causes

explanation: Hemolysis invalidates many tests; recollect and improve technique.

prompt: Ammonia and lactic acid specimens often require:

options.text: Chilling and prompt processing per laboratory protocol
Multi-hour delays at room temperature without issue
Exposure to direct sunlight
Mixing with food residue

explanation: These analytes are unstable; ice slurry and rapid analysis are common requirements.

prompt: Aliquoting a specimen means:

options.text: Drawing arterial blood only
Dividing plasma/serum into secondary tubes for testing/storage
Discarding the accession label
Heating until boiling

explanation: Aliquots allow multiple tests while preserving primary tube integrity rules.

prompt: Cold agglutinins specimens often require:

options.text: Freezing immediately at −70°C at the bedside always
Leaving on ice for 24 hours without order
Keeping the specimen warm (body temperature) as directed until processing
Shaking until frothy

explanation: Cold agglutinins can precipitate in the cold—follow warm-handling instructions.

distractorExplanations.a: Not a routine deep-freeze bedside step.
distractorExplanations.b: Ice may be wrong for this test.

prompt: Which analyte is commonly elevated by prolonged fist clenching/pumping during venipuncture?

options.text: Only urine specific gravity
Only CSF glucose
Room air oxygen percent
Potassium (and other muscle-related changes)

explanation: Muscle activity can raise potassium and affect other results—avoid pumping.

prompt: Bilirubin specimens are often protected from light because light can:

options.text: Degrade bilirubin and falsely lower results
Increase WBC count mechanically
Sterilize the specimen completely
Convert serum to plasma

explanation: Light-sensitive analytes need amber tubes/wraps as specified.

distractorExplanations.b: Light does not raise WBC via that mechanism.
distractorExplanations.c: Light does not sterilize blood tubes.

prompt: A specimen rejected for improper labeling should be:

options.text: Relabeled in the lab based on guesswork
Recollected with correct bedside labeling per policy—do not relabel another patient's tube casually
Poured into a correctly labeled empty tube from a different patient
Reported as normal without testing

explanation: Mislabeled specimens are patient-safety events; recollect rather than guess.

prompt: Which statement about blood culture collection is correct?

options.text: Palpate the site heavily with a nonsterile finger after final prep without reprepping
Collect through a contaminated IV line always as first choice
Cleanse the site thoroughly with appropriate antiseptic and fill bottles to correct volumes without touching sterile bottle tops with nonsterile items
Shake bottles with the butterfly needle still attached in midair

explanation: Aseptic prep and correct volume maximize pathogen recovery and minimize contamination.

distractorExplanations.b: Peripheral draws are preferred when possible.

prompt: The most important step before venipuncture is:

options.text: Identify the patient with two identifiers and verify the order
Ask only 'are you the diabetic?'
Proceed if the wristband is missing and the bed card matches a common name
Skip ID for unconscious patients without using alternative policy

explanation: Wrong-patient draws are critical errors; follow facility ID policy including unconscious patients.

distractorExplanations.d: Unconscious patients still require ID protocol.

prompt: A patient feels faint during a draw. The phlebotomist should:

options.text: Have them stand and walk quickly
Stop the draw, protect from fall, and place them supine if needed
Continue filling all tubes while they are syncope
Give ammonia spirits into both eyes

explanation: Vasovagal syncope needs immediate safety measures and discontinuation as needed.

prompt: Standard Precautions during phlebotomy include:

options.text: No gloves if the patient looks healthy
Washing gloves between patients for reuse
Gloves for draws and hand hygiene before/after glove use
Recapping all needles carefully with two hands

explanation: All blood is potentially infectious; gloves are single-use.

prompt: If a patient refuses a blood draw, the correct action is:

options.text: Hold the patient down and draw anyway
Bribe them without documenting
Draw from a roommate instead
Do not force the procedure; document and notify the appropriate clinician/staff

explanation: Competent refusal must be respected; coercion and wrong-patient draws are unethical/illegal.

distractorExplanations.c: Roommate substitution is a critical ID error.

prompt: Mastectomy on the left side means venipuncture should generally:

options.text: Avoid the left arm (same side) unless a physician specifically approves
Prefer the surgical-side arm for better access
Use only arteries on that side
Ignore the history

explanation: Same-side draws risk lymphedema/infection concerns; choose alternate site per policy.

distractorExplanations.c: Arterial puncture is not the default alternative.

prompt: Iatrogenic anemia is a risk when:

options.text: Only one lavender tube is ever drawn in adults
Excessive cumulative blood volumes are drawn, especially in infants/critically ill
Patients drink water
Tourniquets are used for 30 seconds

explanation: Frequent large draws can cause anemia; pediatric volumes are carefully limited.

distractorExplanations.a: Single adult tubes rarely cause anemia alone.

prompt: Which is appropriate patient communication before a draw?

options.text: Say nothing and grab the arm suddenly
Promise there will be absolutely no discomfort ever
Explain the procedure briefly and obtain consent/cooperation
Tell them the test diagnoses cancer for sure

explanation: Brief honest explanation builds trust; do not guarantee zero pain or over-interpret tests.

prompt: A hematoma forming during venipuncture should prompt:

options.text: Probe deeper blindly while the tourniquet stays tight
Ignore growing swelling
Apply heat immediately and continue drawing
Release tourniquet, remove needle, and apply pressure

explanation: Expanding hematoma means stop and hold pressure to limit bleeding into tissue.

prompt: Hand hygiene should be performed:

options.text: Before and after each patient contact and after glove removal
Only at the start of the shift
Only if visible dirt is present
Never if gloves were worn

explanation: Hand hygiene is required even with glove use because gloves are not perfect.

distractorExplanations.d: Gloves do not replace hand hygiene.

prompt: If stuck by a used needle, you should:

options.text: Continue working without reporting
Wash the area, report exposure immediately, and follow employee health protocol
Suck the wound vigorously
Apply hydrofluoric acid

explanation: Prompt reporting enables timely evaluation/prophylaxis.

prompt: Which patient should generally not have a venipuncture in the arm on the same side as a mastectomy with lymph node dissection (without provider approval)?

options.text: Any patient who ate breakfast
Any patient under age 50 automatically
A patient with lymphedema risk on that side—prefer the opposite arm per policy
Only patients with green eyes

explanation: Avoid compromised lymphatic drainage arms unless cleared—reduces infection/lymphedema risk.

distractorExplanations.a: Food intake is a fasting issue, not mastectomy side rule.
distractorExplanations.b: Age alone is not the rule.

prompt: The best site for an infant heel stick is generally:

options.text: The center of the heel pad deeply
The fingers of a newborn exclusively for all tests
The antecubital fossa with a 15G needle routinely
The lateral or medial plantar surface of the heel—avoid the center/back of the heel

explanation: Approved heel regions reduce calcaneal injury risk; deep central punctures can injure bone.

distractorExplanations.b: Fingers may be used later but heel is common in neonates.
distractorExplanations.c: Large AC needles are inappropriate for infants.

prompt: Transmission-based precautions for a patient with suspected TB include:

options.text: Airborne precautions with appropriate respirator and room controls per facility policy
No PPE if the patient is coughing into a tissue sometimes
Contact gown only without respiratory protection
Shoe covers as the sole control

explanation: TB requires airborne precautions beyond Standard Precautions alone.

distractorExplanations.b: Tissues alone are insufficient for staff protection.
distractorExplanations.c: Gowns do not replace respirators for airborne TB.
distractorExplanations.d: Shoe covers are not the key control.

prompt: A patient refuses a blood draw after education. The phlebotomist should:

options.text: Hold the patient down to obtain the sample
Respect the refusal, notify the appropriate clinician/nurse, and document per policy
Draw anyway because the doctor ordered it
Fabricate results

explanation: Competent refusal must be honored; coercion is assault and unethical.

distractorExplanations.c: Orders do not override refusal capacity rules.

prompt: Which action reduces needlestick risk most effectively?

options.text: Passing uncapped needles hand-to-hand routinely
Leaving sharps on the bed for housekeeping
Using engineered sharps safety devices and disposing immediately in sharps containers without two-handed recapping
Bending needles before disposal

explanation: Safety devices + immediate disposal are core OSHA-aligned practices.
